Raymond CHOO, PhD Candidate in Information Technology of Queensland University of Technology, Australia, presents a talk to our SIS faculty, Information Security cluster, titled Key Establishment -- Proofs and Refutations: An Overview.We study the problem of secure key establishment. We examine the security models of Bellare & Rogaway (1993) and Canetti & Krawczyk (2001) in the computational complexity approach critically, as these models are central in the understanding of the provable security paradigm. We identify several variants of the key sharing requirement (i.e., two entities who have completed matching sessions -- partners, are required to accept the same session key) and present a brief discussion about the key sharing requirement. We identify several variants of the Bellare & Rogaway (1993) model and present a compa-rative study into the relative strengths of security notions between the several variants of the Bellare & Rogaway (1993) model and the Canetti & Krawczyk (2001) model. In our study, we reveal a drawback in the Bellare, Pointcheval, & Rogaway (2000) model, a variant of the Bellare & Rogaway model, with the protocol of Abdalla & Pointcheval (2005) as a case study...

popuptext: Professor Ramayya KRISHNAN, William W. and Ruth F. Cooper Professor of Management Science and Information Systems, presents a talk titled Software Diversity for Information Security. His talk abstract is as follows: In this talk, we argue that considering positive network effects alone leads to over-investment in homogeneity, which in turn results in higher security loss, a cost associated with homogeneity often ignored in the IS and standardization literature. We analyse a software diversification-based strategy to achieve information security. The notion of using diversity to limit correlated risks is a widely accepted strategy in many fields. Various risk management approaches strive to minimise the variance of losses faced by individual firms by either risk pooling, as in insurance, or diversification, as in portfolio management. However, these approaches are advantageous only for risk-averse agents as the expected loss remains unchanged. Exploiting characteristics unique to information systems, we present a framework that allows us to quantify security loss faced by a firm under different technology acquisition strategy. We show that diversification is an effective means to reduce security loss because diversification reduces not only loss variance but also expected loss. The effect of diversification is also explored under different conditions.
visitor_designation: William W. and Ruth F. Cooper Professor of Management Science and Information Systems

popuptext: Professor Miron LIVNY is visiting SIS to discuss his recent research and areas of common interest with SIS faculty. Dr. Livny received a B.Sc. degree in Physics and Mathematics in 1975 from the Hebrew University and M.Sc. and Ph.D. degrees in Computer Science from the Weizmann Institute of Science in 1978 and 1984, respectively. Since 1983 he has been on the Computer Sciences Department faculty at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, where he is currently a Professor of Computer Sciences and is leading the Condor project. Dr. Livnys research focuses on distributed processing and data management systems and data visualisation environments. His recent work includes the Condor high throughput computing system, the DEVise data visualisation and exploration environment and the BMRB repository for data from NMR spectroscopy.
Wenbo MAO, currently a Principal Engineer at Trusted Systems Lab, Hewlett-Packard Laboratories, presented a talk titled "Trusted Computing for GRID Security".

Dr. Hengming ZOU, currently an associate professor of computer science at Shanghai Jiao Tong University, China, presents a talk titled "Reference Modeling for Highly Reliable Software Design". The relentless pursuit of wider applicability and higher performance has resulted in exceedingly large and complex software systems. But how to efficiently build large yet highly reliable software systems remains a persistent challenge faced by IT researchers and practitioners. Many techniques and methods have been proposed to deal with this challenge, but the result of these software engineering efforts is mixed at best and disappointing at worst. Time calls for new solutions. We propose a software reference modeling approach that provides significant reliability enhancement for large and complex software systems and at the same time dramatically reduces development cost and improves development efficiency. In summary, our software reference model revolutionises the development of highly reliable software systems and adds a new horizon to the existing software engineering theories.

Narayan RAMASUBBU, PhD candidate at the Stephen M. Ross School of Business, University of Michigan, presents a talk titled "Managing  globally distributed software development". Distributed software development is becoming pervasive in the software industry. The chief propellers for the explosive growth in adoption of distributed software development model have been the keen intent among firms to leverage global resources and labour cost differences. However academic research in software engineering, virtual teams  and organisation science has cautioned that dispersion could lead to lowered  performance because of increased interdependencies and coordination challenges. In this study I analyse the role of quality management practices in mitigating the effect of dispersion on software project performance.

Dr. SHEN Heng Tao, Lecturer at the School of Information Technology and Electrical Engineering, The University of Queensland, Australia, presents a talk titled "Towards Effective Indexing for Very Large Video Sequence Database". With rapid advances in video processing technologies and ever fast increments in network bandwidth, the popularity of video content publishing and sharing  has made similarity search an indispensable operation to retrieve videos of user interests. The video similarity is usually measured by the percentage of similar  frames shared by two video sequences, and each frame is typically represented as a high-dimensional feature vector. Unfortunately, high complexity of video content has posed the following major challenges for fast retrieval: (a) effective and compact video representations, (b) efficient similarity measurements, and (c) efficient indexing on the compact representations. In this talk, we propose a number of methods to achieve fast similarity search for very large video database.
visitor_designation: Lecturer, School of Information Technology and Electrical Engineering

popuptext: Benjamin GAN, currently an Associate at Institute of System Science (ISS), National University of Singapore, will present a talk titled "Which pedagogical patterns to use for the SMU SIS IT courses?". A popular educational concept based on the constructivism learning theory is problem based learning (PBL). In PBL, the problem drives the learning. There are several characteristics in PBL. One is that it is centered on the student, not the teacher. The PBL concept is effective but requires a paradigm shift. In Singapore, the Republic Polytechnic implements PBL using a one-day-one-problem approach. Should SMU be using this approach? Or should SMU be using other implementations of PBL? Can a ‘hybrid concept exist with PBL? Patterns are solutions to recurring problems in  different context. Pedagogical patterns are solutions to solving educational  problems. Some of these pedagogical patterns are problem based and can be used to implement PBL. How can SIS take advantage of these pedagogical patterns as strategies to improve the teaching of its IT courses? This talk will offer some lights into the questions posed. However, the intention is to discuss experiences of teaching IT courses and how to use PBL.

Michelle CHEONG, currently a PhD student with the Singapore-MIT Alliance (SMA) Program, presented a talk titled New Models in Logistics  Network Design and Implications to 3PL Companies. The research presentation  will focus on two new models in logistics network design and the implications of  the results on 3PL business practice. The first model explores how the 3PL can design a better network by segmenting customers according to their delivery lead time. The benefits and insights of designing a network with segmented  demand, and the results from the application of the model on a chemical industry case study will be discussed. The second model studies the use  of price discount to entice customers to move from one demand segment to the other, resulting in a network design which can improve the profit to run the network and distribute the product. The quantitative benefit will be shown with the application of the model to the chemical industry case study.
visitor_designation: PhD Candidate in Operations Management, Singapore-MIT Alliance (SMA)

popuptext: Professor DENG Xiaotie, currently a Professor at City University of Hong Kong, Department of Computer Science, presented a talk titled Computational Complexity of Pricing Models. The rise of the Internet has brought over business opportunities on a global scale that is not  matched ever before. It also provides a stage for interplays of computer science and economics in a profound scale. In this talk, Prof. DENG discussed the algorithmic approach in economics, including methodologies, issues and challenges. In particular, he focused on the pricing mechanisms (such as arbitrage, auction, and general equilibrium) and their computational complexity issues.

Dr Hemant K. BHARGAVA, Professor at University of California, Davis, Graduate School of Management conducted a workshop titled Research in Information Technology: Opportunities at the interface of OR, Computing and Economics. Dr. BHARGAVA discussed research opportunities in information technology and systems, at (a) the interface of operations research and computing, and (b) at the interface of economics and information systems. This discussion will include several examples from his work and that of colleagues - both successfully published and still in progress. He also discussed leading conferences and journals that publish this sort of work.

Dr. Nilotpal CHAKRAVARTI, a Senior Consultant at ORTEC, one of the worlds largest OR consultancy companies, head-quartered in Holland  presented a talk titled Issues in Revenue Management Implementation. Revenue management (also known as yield management) is the use of differential pricing to optimise revenue. Industries which today use revenue management, include hotels, car rentals, trucking, broadcasting, energy, entertainment, cruises, railways and healthcare. Revenue management leads to impressive revenue improvement with gains of 2-8% being frequently reported. The successful implementation of revenue management requires sophisticated forecasting and optimisation algorithms, careful fine tuning of business processes and use of appropriate information technology interfaces. A variety of theoretical and practical issues arise in this context. The seminar focused on several of these.

ZHU, Kenny Qili, a PhD candidate and teaching assistant at National  University of Singapore presented a talk on an overview of Open  Constraint Programming (OCP). OCP is a new programming paradign that offers shared-memory concurrent programming for reactive systems and applications. The key feature of this paradigm is  that it allows multiple agent programs to interact, react and synchronise with a highly structured shared memory by program fragments called  "reactors" coded in the OCP language. The shared memory represents  both the environment for the agents and a knowledge base. The OCP framework is particularly useful in the areas of e-business, multi-agent  systems, coordinated problem solving, etc. In this talk, he gave an overview on: (1) the OCP reactive programming model; (2) the system  architecture design and the implementation of the first OCP system; (3) an efficient spatial indexing method, RC-tree, which is used as the  key triggering mechanism in the OCP system; (4) several interesting applications of OCP; and (5) the important extension of OCP model to "speculative reaction", a framework that allows the powerful "speculation" in business transactions and other applications..
visitor_designation: Teaching Assistant and PhD Candidate, Department of Computer Science

popuptext: Dr Mark S. FOX, Professor of Industrial Engineering, Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering at University of Toronto Bahen Centre for Information Technology presented a talk on Enterprise Modelling: An Ontological Perspective. As information systems play a  more active role in the management and operations of an enterprise, the demands on the these systems have also increased. Departing from  their traditional role as simple repositories of data, information systems must now provide more sophisticated support to manual and automated decision making; they must not only answer queries with what is explicitly represented in their Enterprise Model, but must be able to answer queries  with what is implied by the model. The goal of the TOVE Enterprise  Modelling project is to create the next generation Enterprise Model, a  Common Sense Enterprise Model. By common sense we mean that an Enterprise Model has the ability to deduce answers to queries that require relatively shallow knowledge of the domain. We have taken an ontological  approach to constructing our Common Sense Enterprise Model. An ontology is a formal description of entities and their properties, relationships, constraints, and behaviours. This talk will review over 20 years of Enterprise Modelling research spanning the representation of activity, state, causality, time, resources, orders, products and organisation.
visitor_designation:
Professor and Head of the Enterprise Integration Laboratory, Department of Industrial Engineering
Co-founder and Chairman/CEO, Novator Systems Ltd

popuptext: Dr. Zheng SUN, Assistant Professor at Hong Kong Baptist University, Department of Computer Science presented a talk titled Algorithmic  Mechanism Design and Internet Economy. Algorithmic mechanism design, an emerging interdisciplinary area of computer science, management science, and economics, studies various game-theoretic scenarios where participants are independent and self-interested agents who may use carefully designed lies to influence the outcome of the game in order to maximise their own gains. The main objective is to design a strategyproof mechanism such that it is the best strategy for each agent to remain truthful. In this talk, he presented a recent work on several problems arising from a variety of application domains, including electronic auctions and network protocol design. He also briefly discussed his work in data mining and, in particular, its applications in electronic commerce. Part of this talk represented joint work with collaborators.
visitor_designation: Assistant Professor, Department of Computer Science

popuptext: John CHUANG, Assistant Professor of Information Management and Systems at the University of California, Berkeley presented a talk titled Economics - Informed Design of Peer-to-Peer Systems. From file-sharing to mobile ad-hoc networks, community networking to application layer overlays, the peer-to-peer networking paradigm promises to revolutionise the way we design, build and use the communications network of tomorrow, transform the structure of the communications industry, and challenge our understanding of markets and democracies in a digital age. The fundamental premise of peer-to-peer systems is that individual peers voluntarily contribute resources to the system. However, the inherent tension between individual rationality and collective welfare produces a misalignment of incentives in the grassroots provisioning of p2p services. In this talk, John discussed the research activities of the p2pecon@berkeley project, where his multi-disciplinary research team is working to develop a framework for understanding the technical and economic characteristics of p2p systems, and to design economic mechanisms for incentive compatible p2p applications.
visitor_designation: Assistant Professor, Information Management and Systems

popuptext: Jason WOODARD, a PhD. Candidate in Engineering and Applied Sciences at Harvard University presented a talk on Architectural Strategy in Complex  Modular Systems. He plans to do three things in this seminar. First, he said  more about architectural strategy and his approach to studying it, including  modeling setup, its relation to existing work, and its novel features. Second,  he provided a snapshot of his dissertation research, focusing on a model of value migration in platform industries that he is about to begin experiments  with. This is work very much in progress; he had more questions than  answers, and had a discussion that sharpened both. Third, he briefly outlined his research agenda for the next several years, and explained how he see it  fitting with the "three pillars" of SIS research and education.

Gene TSUDIK, a Professor and Associate Dean of Research and Graduate Studies in the School of Information and Computer Science at the University of California, Irvine presented a talk titled Integrating Admission Control with P2P and Group  Communication Systems. Peer-to-peer (P2P) security has received a lot of attention as of late. Most prior work focused almost entirely on isses related to secure  communication, such as key management and peer authentication. However, an important pre-requisite for secure communication -- secure peer admission -- has been neither recognised nor adequately addressed. In this talk, he reported on the implementation of BOUNCER, an experimental peer group admission control toolkit and its trial integration with two peer group systems with very different goals and  semantics: Gnutella and Secure Spread. He also discussed some outstanding issues, challenges and future research directions relevant to this topic.
visitor_designation: Professor and Associate Dean of Research and Graduate Studies, School of Information and Computer Science

popuptext: Dr Ajantha DAHANAYAKE, Associate Professor at Delft University of Technology presented a talk on Complex Adaptive Systems for  Process Innovation. The centralised attitudes that dominated the ICT systems engineering principles in the late 20th century, and their solutions are becoming less efficient for todays applications and for the available  technology infrastructures. Well known examples from recent years are the ERP systems, CRM systems and Data Warehousing. All these approaches have been used in an attempt to bring diverse information into a central storage with a predefined data structures to manage and control the solution space. With the increased availability of fast and reliable electronic communication and cheap transport, the coordination of organisational processes distributed over various independent locations has improved significantly. As a result the focus on information handling within and between different data stores requires a more complex  distribution model. E-commerce, GIS and mobile transactions provide some interesting examples. During the presentation a brief overview of the META-CAME research project – which was devoted to computer aided method engineering and BETADE (Building Blocks for distributed Telematics Applications Development and Evaluation) research project will be given, in order to explain the research results of exploring the Component Based Development and its contribution to Complex Adaptive Systems for Process Innovation.
visitor_designation: Associate Professor, Department of System Engineering

popuptext: Dr Anandhi S. BHARADWAJ, Associate Professor at Emory University  presented a talk on IT Outsourcing Contracts: An Empirical Analysis of Contract Imcompleteness. IT outsourcing has experienced tremendous growth in recent years. From an early focus on cost reduction, businesses today expect to gain many advantages from IT outsourcing. The heightened  importance of IT outsourcing to firm success has prompted concerns about the ability of firms to draft efficient outsourcing contracts. IT outsourcing contracts tend to be unavoidably incomplete because it is too costly to  anticipate and describe all relevant contingencies and to pre-specify the exact behaviors of the contracting parties. Relational contracting elements  (e.g. reputation, relational norms, and repeated interactions) are therefore commonly used by firms to safeguard against problems stemming from  contract incompleteness. This paper has two broad motivations. First, we seek to better understand the structure of IT outsourcing contracts. Second, we develop a model of the determinants of contract incompleteness in IT outsourcing.

Dr. LAU Hoong Chuin, Assistant Professor in the School of Computing at National University of Singapore and Head of Defense Logistics Program (TLI-AP) at The Logistics Institute Asia Pacific presented a talk on Defence Logistics - Challenges and Optimization Research. Defense is a key concern in the heightened state of security alert worldwide. Defense logistics is concerned with the management of combat resources and means to sustain military operations. This talk is divided into two parts. The first part is a general discussion of defense logistics challenges from the information systems and decision support perspectives, and an introduction of the JAGUAR research programme at the  Logistics Institute Asia Pacific. In the second part, there will be a discussion on two optimisation problems.
visitor_designation: Assistant Professor (School of Computing) and Head of Defense Logistics Program (TLI-AP), School of Computing and The Logistics Institute Asia Pacific

popuptext: Dr. Kuldeep KUMAR, Professor of Information Systems in the Chapman Graduate School of Business at Florida International  presented a talk on research program on managing globally distributed work. Work is Globally Distributed when it is sub-divided into discrete or overlapping work components, and distributed across work locations and/or organizational units spanning global distances. During this presentation he described a research program for  investigating the challenges in the management of globally distributed work and identifying best practices and solutions to these problems. The presentation will end with an in-depth description of a specific research project focusing on the evolving patterns of interdependencies  and coordination in global work and the design of strategies for managing intense collaboration dependencies in global work.
visitor_designation: Professor of Information Systems, Chapman Graduate School of Business

popuptext: Dr. Nicholas SHEPPARD, research fellow in The University of Wollongong, Australia, School of Information Technology & Computer Science will be presenting a talk on Multimedia Security and Inter-operability of Digital Rights Management Systems. Multimedia security has come to prominence in its role in protecting the intellectual property rights of multimedia content owners. The talk will begin with a brief overview of multimedia security technologies being developed in the Secure Multimedia Information Communications Labs at the University of Wollongong, Australia, including digital watermarking, multimedia authentication and digital rights management.The talk will then detail our work in inter-operability of digital rights management systems.
visitor_designation: Research Fellow, School of Information Technology & Computer Science

popuptext: Dr. LEE Jae Kyu is a Professor and Head of the Management Information Systems Program at the Graduate School of Management. Dr. LEE presented a talk titled Semantic Web Community is concerned about processing the rules implicitly embedded in Web pages that cannot be processed with XML. The implicit rules must be represented in such a way as to allow software agent to process and browse the original Web pages for human comprehension. For this purpose, we have developed the eXtensible Rule Markup Language (XRML), which consists of Rule Identification, Rule Structuring, and Rule Triggering Markup Languages. XRML is applied to the automated form processing, consulting the shipping and return policy, auditing user agreement in Internet shopping malls, and inter-organisational knowledge exchange and integration among heterogeneous organizations. The XRML2.0 approach identifies rules not only from texts, but also from tables on Web pages, and transforms the identified components to the formal rules automatically. While designing XRML2.0, we considered the features of sharing variables and values, omitted terms, and synonyms. An experiment has been conducted to evaluate the potential benefit of the XRML approach with the real world Web pages about the Shipping and Return Policy in Amazon.com, BarnesandNoble.com, and Powells.com. The result provides solid proof that XRML can facilitate the extraction and maintenance of rules from Web pages while building expert systems in the Semantic Web environment.
visitor_designation: Professor and Head of the Management Information Systems Program, Graduate School of Management
visitor_institution: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology

popuptext: Professor Vijay K. VAISHNAVI is currently teaching in the Department of Computer Information Systems at Georgia State University. He presented a talk titled A Novel Approach to facilitate Bio-Informatics Information Integration. The approach is based on the proposition that, by monitoring, extracting, clustering, and visualising bio-informatics metadata across disparately created data sources, patterns of practice can be identified and the definition of standards requirements can be facilitated, thereby promoting homogeneity of data sources. To instantiate the approach, a research architecture, microSEEDS, and its implementation and envisioned uses are discussed.
visitor_designation: Professor, Department of Computer Information Systems

popuptext: ENG Pin Kwang, a computer science doctoral candidate and teaching assistant in the School of Computing at National University of Singapore presented a talk on Skyline/Preference Query Processing. User preferences are invaluable for extracting relevant data that are of specific interests to the user e.g. in web-based data intensive applications as well as decision support systems. Although deriving and modeling user preferences has been extensively studied in recent years, there is still a need for efficient techniques that incorporate user preferences in actual systems. In this talk, he presented several strategies we developed for answering preference queries in relational database systems. All our proposed techniques are able to provide a quick initial response as well as return answers progressively to conserve computational resources. He discussed the tradeoffs in adopting the various approaches and illustrate some results from our experimental studies on these approaches.
visitor_designation: Ph.D. candidate in Computer Science, School of Computing

popuptext: HUANG Yun, a Ph.D. candidate in the Department of Management Science and Information Systems and a research associate at the Center for Research in Electronic Commerce, both at the University of Texas at Austin presented a talk titled Defeating DDoS Attacks by Fixing the Incentive Chain. The Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attack is one of the most sophisticated and dangerous threats against eBusiness infrastructure. Although commercial solutions for DDoS attacks are already available, yet organisations in the best position to implement them lack incentive to do so, and victims of DDoS attacks cannot find effective methods to motivate them.In this talk, HUANG Yun summarised some technological solutions to DDoS attacks, such as cooperative filtering and caching, and then show the root cause of their failures – the broken incentive chain and the lack of global information in the cooperative scheme of defending. As a remedy, he introduced a reputation based approach, which enables victims to disseminate enough incentive along the attack path to stimulate cooperation against DDoS attacks.
visitor_designation:
Ph.D. candidate in Computer Science, Department of Management Science and Information Systems

(The talk is taking place at Carnegie Mellon University)

popuptext:
WANG Fusheng , a Ph.D. candidate, UCLA Department of Computer Science presented a talk titled XML- Based Support for Database Histories and Document Versions. Better support for temporal applications by database systems represents an important technical objective that is difficult to achieve since it requires integrated solutions for technical problems that are challenging on their own. The objective includes:
(i)  expressive temporal representations and data models
(ii) powerful languages for temporal queries and snapshot queries
(iii)indexing, clustering and query optimization techniques for managing temporal information efficiently, and
(iv)architectures that bring together the different pieces of enabling technology into a robust system.

WANG Fusheng presented the ArchIS system that achieves these objectives by supporting a temporary grouped data model on top of RDBMS.
visitor_designation:
Ph.D. candidate in Computer Science

(The talk is taking place at Carnegie Mellon University)

popuptext:
Althea LIANG, a Post-doctoral research staff in the University of Florida presented a talk on Composite Service Discovery, Description and Invocation for Web services. This talk illustrated the following:

1) a semi-automatic approach to composite Web services discovery, description and invocation
2) an intelligent service registry with constraint matching capabilities to support composite service discovery with resilient input requirements and restrictive efforts, and
3) a composite service processor to execute composite services by invoking various component service options.

A search algorithm for constructing the composite service template that represents the discovered composite service were covered.
visitor_designation:
Post-doctoral research staff, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

(The talk is taking place at Carnegie Mellon University)

popuptext:
Dr. Venky SHANKARARAMAN, Associate, National University of Singapore, Institute of Systems Science presented a talk on the Issues in Teaching Enterprise Integration and Architectural Analysis; an important topic that must be covered in Information Systems and Software Engineering courses. The effective teaching of these courses requires thorough planning and proper instructional design that blends theory and real world application and encourage students to synthesize what they learn in the other core subjects. The following two questions will be discussed:

What are the issues in teaching enterprise integration and architecture analysis?

How can one address these issues to make learning more effective?

KIM Dongmin, a Ph.D. candidate in MIS at the University of British Columbia (UBC) presented a talk on The Effects of Trust-Assuring Arguments on Consumer Trust in Internet Stores. The focus of this research is on how to organize and structure the content of trust-assuring arguments in order to increase customers trust.

Dr. Jeff YAN, an assistant professor with the Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Chinese University of Hong Kong presented a talk on Technical mechanisms, human factors and security. Traditionally, security is largely concerned with design of technical mechanisms, and its underlying philosophy is that security fails because of lack or failure of technical mechanisms. Often, however, security also fails because of failures of other issues such as usability, and it should be addressed as a socio-technical system. This is part of the emerging human aspects of security. In this talk, Dr. YAN will showcase his recent work and research style by presenting three projects and their significance; one about systems security, the second about applied crypto and the third about usable security.
visitor_designation: Assistant Professor, Department of Computer Science and Engineering
visitor_institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

popuptext: Dr. Thomas B. HODEL, senior researcher in the University of Zurich, Database Technology Research Group, Department of Informatics presented a talk on Text Native Database eXtension (TeNDaX). Dr. HODEL discussed about the Document business process technologies, native text database, computer supported cooperative work (CSCW), collaborative document processing and web services.
visitor_designation: Senior Researcher, Database Technology Research Group and Department of Informatics

popuptext: Dr. JARZABEK Stanislaw, Associate Professor in National University of Singapore, School of Computing, Department of Computer Science presented a talk on Managing changes in software maintenance and reuse. In the first part of his talk, Dr. JARZABEK discussed the empirical studies on redundancies and their elimination, the reason why unwanted redundancies arise in programs, and why they cannot be unified with suitable reusable solutions using conventional program design techniques. In the second part of his talk, Dr. JARZABEK discussed the "visibility of change" in programs - an issue that has not received much recognition in todays programming paradigms, but critical to understanding software reuse and evolution. The talk ended by briefly reviewing his current projects and future plans, and discussing the potential impact of presented concenpts and findings on research development practice and research.
visitor_designation: Associate Professor, School of Computing, Department of Computer Science

popuptext:
Professor Jerome YEN is currently teaching in the Department of Systems Engineering and Engineering Management at The Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK). During his presentation, he discussed his research in Financial Knowledge Management with agent and XML technologies . The research motivation was to improve the transparency and efficiency through financial information collection, codification, organization, and dissemination, where were the key steps of knowledge management process. Furthermore, he discussed the proposed Financial Knowledge Management and two related projects:
1) ELFFS (ELectronic Financial Filing System)
2) iVIEW (Intelligent Video over InternEt and Wireless channels).
visitor_designation: Professor, Department of Systems Engineering and Engineering Management
visitor_institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

popuptext: Professor Vijay S. MOOKERJEE is currently teaching at University of Texas at Dallas, School of Management. He presented a talk titled, Fault Threshold Policy to manage Software Development Projects. It is a project management policy in which the appearance of software faults during system construction is used to determine the timing of system integration activities (eg. team meetings, analyzing modules for interface inconsistencies, system fault correction etc). He will also share an expression he and his partner had derived for calculating fault thresholds and analyze the policy to reveal the presence of three operating regions.

Mr. ANG Chip Hong, senior engineer in the Institute for Infocomm Research (I2R) presented a talk on Radio Frequency IDentification (RFID). The talk began with a general overview of RFID principal and its application commercially. Mr. Ang discussed the following aspects in their RFID research:
1) Basic principal of RFID
2) Key strength and limitation of RFID
3) RFID applications
4) Future of RFID

Dr. Edward NG, Senior Lecturer at National University of Singapore presented a research talk in SMU SIS. This talk addresses two main questions. The first is, what are the key data management issues arising from Basel II and the second is how academic research can help the banking industry meet Basel II requirements. In conclusion, Dr. NG highlighted the cost of Basel II and a proposal that banks should leverage on the investment made to develop other business solutions that will help them better compete.

Dr. K Subramanian, Senior Deputy Director General at National Informatics Center, Ministry of Communications & Information Technology, New Delhi and also the Information Technology Advisor to CAG of India presented a research talk in SMU SIS. This talk outlines the importance of ISMS, from a management perspective and also the evolution of IT audit and need for a standard curriculum covering the multi-disciplines to produce tomorrows managers.
visitor_institution: National Informatics Center, Ministry of Communications & Information Technology, New Delhi

Dr. Ravi SANDHU was in town to help the School of Information Systems in the planning of security related research and education programs. Dr. SANDHU also visited IDA for dicsussions.
